introSection: Lightning Bolt was a steel roller coaster located at MGM Grand Adventures in Las Vegas, Nevada, USA. It was built by Intamin and opened in 1993, was extended by Arrow Dynamics in 1997 (with the ride's new track and supports engineered by Ride Centerline ), and closed in 2000.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Lightning Bolt was originally built as a long indoor roller coaster with a  lift hill. In 1997, the ride was relocated to an outside location within the park but retained the same layout. Later that same year, a second, lift hill was added and the ride was extended outdoors. The length was increased from to  by Arrow Dynamics. The additional track and second lift hill were built over the Grand Canyon Rapids ride.

The park formally closed to the public on September 4, 2000, but Lightning Bolt was listed as one of three rides that would be retained with the park's transformation into a private event space.

The ride remained standing but not operating through at least December 2002.

After the ride's closure, it was listed for sale online for $660,000 USD. The ride was later sold to Granite Park, a theme park project that was ultimately cancelled, with the coaster ultimately ending up with a scrap dealer.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Pale yellow track and brown supports.

fullText:
Lightning Bolt was a steel roller coaster located at MGM Grand Adventures in Las Vegas, Nevada, USA. It was built by Intamin and opened in 1993, was extended by Arrow Dynamics in 1997 (with the ride's new track and supports engineered by Ride Centerline ), and closed in 2000.

Lightning Bolt was originally built as a long indoor roller coaster with a  lift hill. In 1997, the ride was relocated to an outside location within the park but retained the same layout. Later that same year, a second, lift hill was added and the ride was extended outdoors. The length was increased from to  by Arrow Dynamics. The additional track and second lift hill were built over the Grand Canyon Rapids ride.

The park formally closed to the public on September 4, 2000, but Lightning Bolt was listed as one of three rides that would be retained with the park's transformation into a private event space.

The ride remained standing but not operating through at least December 2002.

After the ride's closure, it was listed for sale online for $660,000 USD. The ride was later sold to Granite Park, a theme park project that was ultimately cancelled, with the coaster ultimately ending up with a scrap dealer.



Pale yellow track and brown supports.
